tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

Currently, two types of queries are supported:

  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.

## DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

### Overview

<cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> Often referred to as the "Decentralized Operating System," <cite index="3-1">DXOS is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud.</cite>

### Company Details

- **Founded:** <cite index="3-5">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="3-6">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Status:** <cite index="3-8">DXOS is a Private company.</cite>
- **Industry:** <cite index="3-7">DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.</cite>
- **Community:** <cite index="31-7">The community spans 8 cities worldwide, with 30+ contributors and 200+ community members.</cite>
- **Team:** <cite index="6-6">DXOS was created by a small core team of developers from many countries.</cite>
- **Investors:** <cite index="3-10">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>

### Mission

<cite index="8-3">DXOS — The decentralized operating system — is an independent public organization whose mission is to provide open source technologies that allow for a free and open decentralized internet.</cite> <cite index="8-6,8-7">The DXOS platform consists of decentralized protocols, tools, and applications that provide an alternative to the current Web. DXOS enables the development of privacy-preserving applications for its users, and a fairer marketplace for open source developers.</cite>

### Core Technology Stack

DXOS is built around three primary protocols/components:

1. **ECHO (Data):** <cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite>

2. **HALO (Identity):** <cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite>

3. **MESH (Network):** <cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite>

### Technical Details

- **Language:** <cite index="4-4">DXOS provides a TypeScript implementation of the DXOS protocols, SDK, toolchain and Composer.</cite>
- **CRDT Support:** <cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite>
- **Networking:** <cite index="1-19">It automatically synchronizes data across networked devices using WebRTC for cloudless peer-to-peer networking.</cite>
- **Architecture:** <cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>
- **APIs:** <cite index="5-5,5-6">DXOS works in any Node.js or Browser environment. There is a TypeScript API and a React API.</cite>
- **Reactive UI:** <cite index="1-20">It re-renders UI components automatically as the underlying data changes, with minimal impact on performance.</cite>

### Composer — Flagship Application

<cite index="37-1">Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.</cite> <cite index="10-6">Composer is a local-first workspace that is designed for small team collaboration.</cite>

Key Composer features include:
- **Documents:** <cite index="31-17">Realtime collaboration inside of a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- **Sheets:** <cite index="35-12,35-13">Supports 300+ basic calculations along with support for custom functions. Extendable via API calls and custom script integrations.</cite>
- **Sketching:** <cite index="36-2">The Sketch plugin allows you to leverage a digital whiteboard and diagram editor powered by TLDraw.</cite>
- **Plugins:** <cite index="31-39,31-40">Everything inside of Composer is a plugin. This means you can leverage core functionality or build out whatever you need.</cite>
- **AI Integration:** <cite index="31-29">Leverage machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting your intellectual property.</cite>
- **Data Ownership:** <cite index="31-1,31-2">You own everything inside of Composer. No spyware, no algorithms, no lock-in.</cite>
- **Import/Export:** <cite index="31-51">Import and export your entire database as plain, human readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>
- **Agents:** <cite index="9-2">DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents.</cite>

### Developer Experience

<cite index="6-12">DXOS wraps its three main components together in a set of SDKs (currently, React and TypeScript) that enable developers to deliver sophisticated applications quickly.</cite>

The SDK includes:
- A `@dxos/client` npm package for TypeScript
- A `@dxos/react-client` package with React hooks
- Chrome DevTools extension for debugging
- Vite as the recommended bundler
- Project templates and configuration examples

### Organizational Structure

<cite index="8-4,8-5">DXOS.org is a decentralized global community of open source developers. The community is organized via an independent foundation (DXOS.foundation), which is funded by members of the community.</cite>

The foundation organizes efforts around three teams:
- **DXOS Protocols:** <cite index="8-15">Development and support of decentralized protocols that initially include: HALO (identity), ECHO (data), and MESH (network).</cite>
- **DXOS Platform:** <cite index="8-17,8-18">Development and operation of a permissionless blockchain (DXNS) and the peer-to-peer KUBE network that provides the operational platform for DXOS applications.</cite>
- **Application Showcase:** Building flagship apps like Composer to demonstrate platform capabilities.

### Current Status

<cite index="37-2,37-3">Composer is not yet ready for production. There are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.</cite> <cite index="33-5">The project is open-source under the MIT license.</cite> <cite index="2-2">DXOS has 64 repositories available on GitHub.</cite>

### Key Links
- **Website:** https://www.dxos.org/
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org/
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos/dxos
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org/
- **Composer App:** https://www.dxos.org/composer

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.text: Let me organize the available typenames from the schemas list.

Here are all the available typenames in your space:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|

| 3 | **`org.dxos.type.feed`** | Data feeds with name, kind, and namespace (data/trace) |
| 4 | **`org.dxos.type.function`** | Registered functions with input/output schemas, versioning, and bindings |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.organization`** | Organizations with name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), website, image, and location |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.person`** | People/contacts with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, organization, URLs, birthday, and more |
| 7 | **`org.dxos.type.prompt`** | Prompt definitions with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context |
| 8 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | Tags with a label and optional hue (color), used to tag other objects |
| 9 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | Event triggers (email, queue, subscription, timer, webhook) that invoke functions |
| 10 | **`org.dxos.type.view`** | Saved views/queries with projections and field configurations |

content.text: Let me organize the available typenames from the schemas list.

Here are all the available typenames in your space:

| # | Typename | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **`com.example.type.expando`** | A flexible/generic object type that allows arbitrary additional properties |
| 2 | **`org.dxos.type.blueprint`** | AI assistant blueprints with instructions, tools, and MCP server configurations |
| 3 | **`org.dxos.type.feed`** | Data feeds with name, kind, and namespace (data/trace) |
| 4 | **`org.dxos.type.function`** | Registered functions with input/output schemas, versioning, and bindings |
| 5 | **`org.dxos.type.organization`** | Organizations with name, description, status (prospect/qualified/active/commit/reject), website, image, and location |
| 6 | **`org.dxos.type.person`** | People/contacts with full name, emails, phone numbers, addresses, job title, organization, URLs, birthday, and more |
| 7 | **`org.dxos.type.prompt`** | Prompt definitions with input/output JSON schemas, instructions, blueprints, and context |
| 8 | **`org.dxos.type.tag`** | Tags with a label and optional hue (color), used to tag other objects |
| 9 | **`org.dxos.type.trigger`** | Event triggers (email, queue, subscription, timer, webhook) that invoke functions |
| 10 | **`org.dxos.type.view`** | Saved views/queries with projections and field configurations |

In total, there are **10 schemas** available. The most commonly used ones for data entry are likely **Person**, **Organization**, **Tag**, and **Expando** (for freeform data). The others are more system/configuration-oriented (blueprints, functions, triggers, views, prompts, feeds).

Would you like to explore any of these in more detail or create objects using any of them?

# DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System

## Overview

<cite index="1-3">DXOS is an open source framework for building real-time, collaborative web applications that run entirely on the client and communicate peer-to-peer, without the need for centralized servers.</cite> <cite index="8-3">DXOS — The Decentralized Operating System — is an independent public organization whose mission is to provide open source technologies that allow for a free and open decentralized internet.</cite>

<cite index="2-3,2-4">The company is a developer of a decentralized platform designed to create the first functionally and commercially competitive, fully decentralized alternative to the corporate cloud. It offers a network operating system, infrastructure platform, programming framework, and toolchain, enabling businesses to develop privacy-preserving applications for its users and a fairer marketplace for open-source developers.</cite>

## Company Details

- **Founded:** <cite index="2-5">DXOS was founded in 2021.</cite>
- **Headquarters:** <cite index="2-6">DXOS is headquartered in New York, NY.</cite>
- **Type:** <cite index="2-8">DXOS is a Private company.</cite>
- **Industry:** <cite index="2-7">DXOS's primary industry is Software Development Applications.</cite>
- **Size:** <cite index="14-2">11-50 employees.</cite>
- **Community:** <cite index="32-5">30+ contributors and 200+ community members across 8 cities worldwide.</cite>
- **Investors:** <cite index="2-10">Backed VC, BlueYard Capital, Empty Set Group, and PL Capital (United States) have invested in DXOS.</cite>
- **License:** <cite index="33-5">Open-source under the MIT license.</cite>

## Organization & Community

<cite index="8-4,8-5">DXOS.org is a decentralized global community of open source developers. The community is organized via an independent foundation (DXOS.foundation), which is funded by members of the community.</cite> <cite index="8-8,8-9">The DXOS community consists of a number of independent decentralized autonomous organizations (DAOs). DAOs may consist of individuals or teams of software developers and other professionals who are contributing to the ecosystem.</cite>

<cite index="30-3,30-4">Braneframe is a Web3 company building peer-to-peer privacy-preserving applications built on open-source protocols. It is a leading project in the DXOS (Decentralized Operating System) ecosystem, whose participants are working toward an alternative to the tech giant-dominated public cloud.</cite>

## Core Technology & Architecture

DXOS is built around three core decentralized protocols:

### 1. ECHO (Data)
<cite index="1-6">ECHO is an open source database architecture that incorporates transparent data replication and conflict resolution for secure and scalable local storage.</cite> <cite index="44-11,44-12">DXOS offers ECHO, a CRDT-based graph database that works entirely on the client. ECHO supports real-time multiplayer and offline writers, and works without a central server or authority — all peers are equal.</cite>

### 2. HALO (Identity)
<cite index="1-7">HALO seamlessly manages digital identities using public/private key pairs allowing nuanced control over access to applications, databases, and networked devices.</cite> <cite index="43-7">HALO is a decentralized identity and access control protocol that makes it easier to build collaborative applications.</cite>

### 3. MESH (Network)
<cite index="1-9">MESH extends existing internet protocols to enable secure and resilient peer-to-peer networks without the need for centralized server infrastructure.</cite>

### How It Works
<cite index="1-11,1-12">DXOS leverages Automerge — a powerful CRDT that enables multiple concurrent users to operate on the same data without loss or conflict.</cite> <cite index="1-14,1-15">The only reason you need a central server is to serve your application shell. Everything else happens in the client and works peer-to-peer.</cite>

<cite index="44-22,44-23,44-24,44-25">Data exchange between peers happens over direct WebRTC connections, which are established via a signaling service for peer discovery. This signaling service doesn't need to handle too much traffic, since peer discovery is very lightweight and all of the actual data is sent peer-to-peer. The signaling service also works as another peer-to-peer network and uses libp2p pubsub (gossipsub) to scale horizontally. DXOS operates a default signaling network, but anyone is welcome to operate their own using their components.</cite>

<cite index="44-26">Overall, this architecture preserves end-user privacy and cuts infrastructure costs significantly, while enabling offline and real-time collaboration.</cite>

## Developer SDK

<cite index="3-1">DXOS is the developer platform for collaborative, local-first, privacy-preserving software.</cite> <cite index="3-5,3-6">DXOS works in any Node.js or Browser environment. There is a TypeScript API and a React API.</cite>

<cite index="12-3,12-4">DXOS provides developers with a toolkit to build local-first, collaborative apps which preserve privacy by communicating peer-to-peer. DXOS applications work offline, share state instantly when online, and leave end-users in control of their data and privacy.</cite>

The SDK also includes:
- <cite index="50-3">EDGE — Web services running on Cloudflare workers, providing sync, backup, compute, etc.</cite>
- <cite index="50-4">App Framework — Build modular applications based around plugins & capabilities, such as Composer.</cite>

<cite index="3-27,3-28">DXOS recommends Vite as the bundler. Vite requires a plugin in order to serve the WebAssembly modules.</cite>

## Flagship Application: Composer

<cite index="5-2">DXOS is also the home of Composer — an extensible IDE, built for both real time and async collaboration.</cite>

<cite index="36-1">Composer is a collaborative productivity app where developers can build and organize knowledge, extend with custom data and UI, and run private AI against their knowledge locally.</cite>

<cite index="31-16">Composer blends the power of popular file formats (docs, sheets, sketch & tables) while allowing you to build plugins, deploy custom functions and leverage modern LLMs.</cite>

### Composer Features:
- <cite index="31-17">Real-time collaboration inside of a modern markdown editor.</cite>
- <cite index="31-19">Threaded comments across multiple document formats.</cite>
- <cite index="31-21">Custom plugins to enhance functionality.</cite>
- <cite index="31-23">Custom schemas to work with structured data.</cite>
- <cite index="31-25">Local-first spreadsheet that supports custom functions.</cite>
- <cite index="31-26">Diagrams and sketches embedded in programmable workflows.</cite>
- <cite index="31-29">Machine learning to build agentic workflows that enhance team productivity while protecting intellectual property.</cite>
- <cite index="31-36">Custom agents that use Composer data as a dynamic context window.</cite>
- <cite index="31-51">Import and export of the entire database as plain, human readable markdown, JSON, and media files.</cite>
- <cite index="38-2">Sketch plugin with a digital whiteboard and diagram editor powered by TLDraw.</cite>
- <cite index="38-4">Built-in voice/video meetings with real-time transcription, task extraction & semantic CRM enhancements.</cite>
- <cite index="38-8">Kanban view to explore Table data in sorted columns defined by custom schema.</cite>

### Privacy in Composer
<cite index="18-4,18-5">Composer is built on the peer-to-peer protocols of DXOS which are designed to be private by default. The data is stored on your device and only shared with others you explicitly invite.</cite> <cite index="31-1,31-2">You own everything inside of Composer. No spyware, no algorithms, no lock-in.</cite>

<cite index="34-1,34-2">Composer is built with extensibility at its core. Everything within the application is built using the same App Framework APIs, from the UI down to data and business logic; anything in Composer can be customized and enhanced.</cite>

**Note:** <cite index="36-2,36-3">Composer is not yet ready for production. There are bugs, and breaking changes may occur.</cite>

## Agents (Managed Service)

<cite index="41-1,41-2">DXOS is launching a fully-managed service to host agents. DXOS is launching a fully-managed service that runs your agents without having to worry about the underlying infrastructure and uptime.</cite>

<cite index="41-3,41-4">The other big reason for choosing the managed service besides great uptime, network speeds, and managed disk space, is it's a simple and convenient offsite backup for your ECHO data and HALO identity. This allows you to recover your account from anywhere in the world, even if you lose all your devices.</cite>

<cite index="41-5,41-6,41-7">Their vision is to enable significant savings through the interoperability of DXOS applications. Local-first apps can share the same agent and data storage, maintaining data privacy without extra charges for each additional app. Similar to iCloud storage, which allows various applications to write to a single data storage layer, this storage belongs entirely to you and can be run anywhere.</cite>

<cite index="18-9,18-10">Agents are DXOS instances running without an attached user interface. A common use of agents is to run on a server and provide availability if all other devices are offline, but this is completely optional.</cite>

## Why "Cloudless"?

<cite index="44-6,44-7,44-8,44-9">This is why they built DXOS. They think end users deserve better. Offline and real-time collaboration should be the default, not the exception. End-user privacy should not have to be surrendered to servers and cloud operators.</cite>

<cite index="44-28">Developers won't have to operate any servers or databases — their app can work entirely on the client, users will be able to work offline, collaborate in real-time, and their data will never leave devices they trust.</cite>

## Key Links

- **Website:** https://www.dxos.org/
- **Documentation:** https://docs.dxos.org/
- **GitHub:** https://github.com/dxos/dxos (64 repositories)
- **Blog:** https://blog.dxos.org/
- **Composer:** https://www.dxos.org/composer
- **Discord:** Community discussion and support
- **Bluesky:** https://bsky.app/profile/dxos.org
- **LinkedIn:** https://www.linkedin.com/company/dxos-org
